title: Gaganyaan nears liftoff, YouTube rewrites the payout rules, and a smart-ring showdown
intro: Today's five: ISRO clears three key Gaganyaan tests, YouTube's new 'inauthentic content' payout rules, a hidden iPad typing trick, smart ring vs smartwatch, and whether you'd trust an AI agent with your card.

posts.hook: India's first spaceflight test is almost here
posts.body: ISRO is quietly ticking every box before India's first Gaganyaan flight. This month it cleared three major qualification tests — the parachute system, the flotation unit that rights the crew module after splashdown, and the mechanism that separates the crew and service modules. The plan from here: G1 flies uncrewed around late 2026, G2 carries Vyommitra — a humanoid robot acting as a stand-in astronaut — and the first crewed flight is targeted for 2027. 🚀 No hype, just steady engineering — exactly how you send humans to space safely.

posts.hook: YouTube is changing what earns money
posts.body: If you make videos, this one matters. YouTube renamed its 'repetitious content' policy to 'inauthentic content', and the shift is bigger than the name. Reviewers and automated systems now check whether a video shows meaningful original effort, whether you're the real voice behind the channel, and whether your niche is full of recycled material. The algorithm has also moved from rewarding 'what keeps people watching longest' to 'what leaves people most satisfied.' The practical result: faceless, templated, copy-paste and lazily AI-generated channels face a slower path into monetization, while original point of view wins. 🎥

posts.hook: Type numbers on iPad without the 123 key
posts.body: Here's an iPad keyboard habit worth building. Instead of tapping the '123' key, just touch any letter and flick down — it instantly types the number or symbol printed on that key. Want the '@'? Flick down on the 'A'. It's called Key Flicks, it's on by default under Settings > General > Keyboard, and it makes typing emails, passwords and numbers noticeably faster. 💡

Smart ring or smartwatch in 2026? A smart ring gives you multi-day battery, excellent sleep and recovery tracking, and a discreet form factor with no screen to distract you — but no notifications and limited features. A smartwatch is a wrist computer: notifications, calls, workouts, apps and tap-to-pay — at the cost of near-daily charging and another screen buzzing at you. The simple rule: buy the ring if you want insight about your body without distraction, and the watch if you want control and convenience on your wrist. 💍⌚️

posts.hook: Would you let an AI agent pay with your card?
posts.body: AI agents are getting good enough to browse, book and pay for things on your behalf — flights, groceries, subscriptions and more. The technology mostly works; trust is the harder part. Would you hand your card to an AI agent to automate the boring stuff, keep it to small low-stakes buys, or avoid it entirely? 🤖
